name: La Roda to Fuensanta Circular Trail
description: This difficult 12.6-mile (20.3 km) circular hiking trail from La Roda to Fuensanta offers cultural landmarks and accessible paths.
region_intro: Hiking around La Roda offers diverse landscapes, from the flat plains characteristic of the La Mancha Alta Albaceteña region to higher elevations exceeding 850 meters (2,788 feet) in the southwest. The area features open countryside and river bends, such as those found in the Hoces del Batanejo. These natural features provide varied terrain for outdoor activities. Best hiking trails around La Roda The most popular hiking route is Route La Marmota — circular, a 5.9 miles (9.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 30 minutes to complete. This moderate route features accessible paths and an elevation gain of 84 meters. Another top favourite among local hikers is Batanejo Gorges Trail, a moderate 10.0 miles (16.1 km) path. This trail winds through scenic river gorges, offering an elevation gain of 76 meters. Local hikers also love the La Roda to Fuensanta Circular Trail, a 12.6 miles (20.3 km) trail leading through varied countryside, often completed in about 5 hours 12 minutes. Hiking around La Roda is defined by open countryside, river gorges, and elevated areas, offering a mix of flat terrain and gentle ascents. The network provides options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to different ability levels. The routes in La Roda are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from 7 reviews. More than 40 hikers have used komoot to explore La Roda's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around La Roda?
A: There are over 15 hiking trails around La Roda, offering a variety of experiences. These include routes through open countryside, along river gorges, and paths that reach higher elevations.

Q: What types of terrain can I expect on hikes in La Roda?
A: Hiking around La Roda features diverse landscapes. You'll find flat plains characteristic of the La Mancha Alta Albaceteña region, as well as areas with gentle ascents reaching over 850 meters (2,788 feet) in the southwest. The region also includes scenic river bends and gorges, such as those found along the Hoces del Batanejo.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners in La Roda?
A: Yes, La Roda offers several easy hiking routes. Out of the 15 available trails, 6 are classified as easy, making them suitable for beginners or those looking for a more relaxed walk.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes in La Roda?
A: Yes, La Roda has several circular hiking routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38160212" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hondo del Río Park – Palacio de los Gosálvez loop from Villalgordo del Júcar&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 14.3 km circular trail. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130210" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Morra Geodetic Vertex loop from Casas de Molina&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 12.5 km route.

Q: What interesting landmarks or natural features can I see while hiking in La Roda?
A: While hiking in La Roda, you can explore various points of interest. The area itself is known for its beautiful countryside. You might encounter historical sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5842894" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Palace of the Counts of Villaleal&lt;/a&gt; or religious buildings such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5454432" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Martín&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/La_Roda" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;town of La Roda&lt;/a&gt; itself is a charming place to explore.

Q: Is La Roda part of any long-distance walking paths?
A: Yes, La Roda is a segment of the historic Camino de Santiago de Levante. This pilgrimage route connects Valencia with Zamora and passes through several municipalities in the province of Albacete, including La Roda, offering a unique long-distance trekking experience.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in La Roda?
A: The hiking trails in La Roda are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.1 stars from 7 reviews. Over 40 hikers have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the open countryside and river gorges.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, for experienced trekkers seeking a challenge, there is at least one difficult route available. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1179543340/la-roda-to-fuensanta-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Roda to Fuensanta Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a 20.3 km route that takes approximately 5 hours and 12 minutes to complete, leading through varied countryside.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in La Roda?
A: While specific seasonal recommendations are not detailed, the region's diverse landscapes suggest that spring and autumn would likely offer the most pleasant hiking conditions, with milder temperatures and vibrant scenery. Summer can be hot, and winter might bring cooler, potentially wetter weather.

Q: Are the hiking trails in La Roda suitable for families?
A: Many of the trails in La Roda are suitable for families, especially the 6 routes classified as easy. The varied terrain, from flat plains to gentle ascents, provides options for different fitness levels, making it possible to find a suitable path for a family outing.

Q: Is public transport available to reach hiking trailheads in La Roda?
A: Information on specific public transport routes to trailheads is not readily available. However, La Roda is a town with connections, so it's advisable to check local bus schedules or consider driving to access the starting points of the trails.
tips:
Dating from the 18th century, it was built from an old 16th-century building by the Countess of Villaleal's grandfather, Doña Joaquina de Arce y Lara, who belonged to the Order of Calatrava.

The house presents rococo decoration motifs and on its façade there is a wavy balcony with a beautiful wrought iron railing; On the very ornate hole in the wall there is a beautiful Baroque coat of arms belonging to the 18th century.

Work qualified as a strange Renaissance piece due to the arrangement of its façade in the form of a corner. The date of his execution, 1627, is recorded on a scroll.

Originally this façade was located in the house located opposite, demolished in 1960 and rebuilt in 1995 by the "La Enzina" Workshop School, placing its ashlars, balcony and shield in front of the original location as if it were the reflection of a mirror.

As a historical note, note that the Queen of Spain Doña María Ana de Austria, widow of Felipe IV, stayed in this house in 1668.

The church of El Salvador de la Roda is a mixture of Gothic and Renaissance styles, the result of the time in which it was built throughout the 16th century and a good part of the 17th. It rises on a promontory in the center of the town, which makes it visible from all its points.

The exterior has a defensive and powerful aspect with large smooth walls modulated by prismatic buttresses without a slope and small openings that serve for internal lighting.

faqs.question: What is the overall difficulty of the La Roda to Fuensanta — circular trail?
faqs.answer: This route is categorized as a hard hike, requiring a very good level of fitness due to its length of 20.3 kilometers and estimated duration of over 5 hours. While some sections may feature easily accessible paths, the overall challenge is significant.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the La Roda to Fuensanta — circular is a loop trail, meaning you will end your hike at or near your starting point without needing to retrace your steps.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this hike?
faqs.answer: The paths along this 20.3 km circular route are generally accessible. The trail passes through the urban area of Fuensanta, so you can expect a mix of natural paths and sections within the town.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The trail features several notable landmarks. In La Roda, you can see the Palace of the Counts of Villaleal and the Church of El Salvador de La Roda. As you pass through Fuensanta, look out for the Arco del Término, the Hermitage of San Antón, and the Sanctuary of Nuestra Señora de Los Remedios. You'll also encounter the Alcañabate Corner.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other established trails. Approximately 12% of the path follows parts of the Camino de Santiago de Levante and the Camino de Santiago del Sureste. Smaller portions also coincide with the Colada del Rio and the Paseo de la Estación.

faqs.question: Is the La Roda to Fuensanta — circular trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Information regarding specific dog restrictions for this trail is not available. However, as a general guideline for longer hikes, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially when passing through urban areas or near wildlife, and to ensure they are fit enough for a challenging 20+ km route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to hike this trail would generally be during spring or autumn when temperatures are milder. Summers in this region can be very hot, making a 20.3 km hike particularly challenging. Winters might be suitable, but always check local weather conditions before you go.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to hike this route?
faqs.answer: There is no indication that permits or entrance fees are required to access the La Roda to Fuensanta — circular trail. It appears to be freely accessible.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Specific parking information for the trailhead is not detailed in the available data. However, as the trail starts and ends in La Roda, it is likely that parking can be found within the town or near the starting point. It's advisable to check local maps or resources for public parking options in La Roda before your visit.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities or places to rest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the trail passes through the urban area of Fuensanta, which means you will find amenities such as shops, cafes, or restaurants there. La Roda, as the starting and ending point, also offers various services and facilities.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a hike of this difficulty and length?
faqs.answer: Given the 'hard' difficulty rating and 20.3 km length, you should bring plenty of water, snacks, appropriate hiking footwear, sun protection (hat, sunscreen), and layers of clothing suitable for the weather. A first-aid kit and a fully charged phone are also highly recommended.
